macro_segments.title: Introduction to Perek IV and the Markolis Discussion

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and transition to Perek IV
Identification and context of Markolis as a roadside idol

macro_segments.title: Introduction to Perek IV and the Markolis Discussion (continued)

micro_segments.title: Description of three-stone configuration and worship method

macro_segments.title: Mishnah on Markolis: Rabbi Ishmael vs. the Sages on Stone Configuration

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Ishmael's position: three stones forbidden even if not configured as pyramid
The Sages' position: two stones permissible; connection to shrine determines status

macro_segments.title: Gemara Analysis: Evidence that stones fell from the Markolis versus independent configuration

micro_segments.title: Explanation of Rav Yosef bar Yosef's ruling on definitively fallen stones

macro_segments.title: Mishnah on Markolis: Rabbi Ishmael vs. the Sages on Stone Configuration (continued)

micro_segments.title: Explanation of proximity principle and the four-amah boundary

macro_segments.title: Gemara Analysis: Evidence that stones fell from the Markolis versus independent configuration (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rava's reinterpretation: stones found nearby presume they fell, not necessarily certain
Introduction of tfisa achat concept: distinction between unified and separated units

macro_segments.title: Parameters of Forbidden Status: Barriers, Distance, and Mini-Markolises

micro_segments.title: Definition of tfisa achat: no mound of dirt separating the stones
Application to meat and milk at the table and evidence from Chullin
Tosafot's reading: restrictions apply within four amot with or without barrier
Gemara evidence that mini-Markolises were made near major shrines

macro_segments.title: Historical Account: Yannai's Palace, Non-Jewish Paving, and the Status of Stones

micro_segments.title: Non-Jews destroyed the palace and created a Markolis; other non-Jews paved roads with the stones
Dispute among Rabbis: some refrained from using the roads, others permitted it
Rabbi Yochanan's ruling: even the righteous walked on these roads

macro_segments.title: Tikrovet Avodah Zarah: Offerings Cannot Be Nullified and the Halakhic Distinction

micro_segments.title: Principle that tikrovet (offerings) have no eternal bittul, unlike the idol itself
Concern that stones from Markolis worship may be tikrovet rather than bittul-able avodah zarah

macro_segments.title: The Braita on Non-Jewish Destruction versus Jewish Destruction of Markolis Stones

micro_segments.title: Braita: non-Jew who paved roads with Markolis stones—permitted; Jew who did so—forbidden
Initial difficulty: why should a non-Jew be able to nullify what a Jew cannot

macro_segments.title: The Braita on Non-Jewish Destruction versus Jewish Destruction of Markolis Stones (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rav Sheshet's explanation: stones are not actual tikrovet, so bittul is possible

macro_segments.title: Tikrovet Avodah Zarah: Offerings Cannot Be Nullified and the Halakhic Distinction (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rav Sheshet's resolution: stones are not tikrovet because not ke'ein panim like Temple offerings

macro_segments.title: Digression on Shimita and Chol HaMoed: When Tree Maintenance Is Permitted or Forbidden

micro_segments.title: Braita on deworming (zizhum) and bark attachment permitted on shimita but forbidden on chol hamoed
First difficulty: why permit tree protection (zizhum) on shimita but forbid pruning (gizum)
Resolution: zizhum prevents tree loss; gizum promotes growth—hence permissibility differs
Second difficulty: why permit oil on cut branches on chol hamoed but forbid oil on unripe fruit
Resolution attempt: branch oil protects exposed area; fruit oil fattens—different categories
Unresolved question from Rav Sama: why are both maintenance acts not treated consistently within chol hamoed

macro_segments.title: Acts of Worship and Substitute Objects: Breaking and Throwing the Stick

micro_segments.title: Teaching: if an avodah zarah is normally worshipped with a stick, breaking the stick is considered worship
Breaking the stick = chayav (liable); throwing the stick = patur (exempt)
Rav Yehuda's logic: breaking resembles shechita (slaughter); throwing does not resemble zrikat hadam (blood sprinkling)
Abaye's challenge: why should throwing not be equivalent to zrikat hadam
Response: blood splatters (mishtaberet); a thrown stick merely falls—insufficient resemblance
